text.title: Backlog Management Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules every Backlog Management application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Backlog MUST follow DEEP: Detailed at top, Emergent at bottom, Estimated for Ready items, Prioritised explicitly per item.
rule.rationale: Treating bottom-of-backlog items with the same detail as top items wastes refinement time.

rule.statement: Every Ready item MUST satisfy INVEST (Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable); failing items are pushed back to Upcoming.
rule.rationale: Items that fail INVEST become sprint killers — block other work, cannot complete in cycle, fail review.

rule.statement: Exactly four buckets: Ready (1-2 sprints), Upcoming (1-3 sprints), Backlog (future), Icebox (parked). Extra buckets are rejected.
rule.rationale: All-items-look-equal kills planning; promotion criteria gate movement.

rule.statement: Every item MUST carry exactly one type tag in {feature, bug, tech_debt, research}; multi-typed items are rejected.
rule.rationale: Type tags drive capacity allocation (feature vs tech debt ratio) and triage automation.

rule.statement: Every item MUST link to its origin (ticket URL, customer quote, dashboard chart); sourceless items are archive candidates at next grooming.
rule.rationale: Sourceless items cannot be validated or traced back when stakeholders ask 'why did we build this?'

rule.statement: Items inactive for 180+ days MUST be auto-proposed for archive (not deleted) — human approves before removal if linked PRs or customer commitments exist.
rule.rationale: 180-day rule provides objective threshold avoiding 'nobody dares delete' paralysis; human gate prevents commitment loss.
